name: Litany of Re
shortDescription: An ancient Egyptian hymn praising the sun god Re in his various forms and aspects, recited to ensure his continued journey and the world's renewal.
description: The Litany of Re, also known as the "Book of the Adoration of Re in the West," is a significant ancient Egyptian funerary text and hymn dedicated to the sun god Re. It is a complex composition found primarily in royal tombs of the New Kingdom period (c. 1550-1070 BCE), most notably in the Valley of the Kings. The text is divided into two parts, with the first part focusing on Re's journey through the twelve hours of the night in the underworld (Duat), and the second part detailing Re's seventy-five forms or aspects. The purpose of the Litany was to ensure the pharaoh's successful journey through the afterlife by merging with Re, thus achieving immortality. Reciting the Litany was believed to empower the deceased king, granting him the sun god's strength and ability to overcome the dangers of the underworld. It reveals much about Egyptian cosmology, beliefs about death and rebirth, and the central role of the sun god in maintaining cosmic order.
subtitle: A Journey Through the Night, A Song of Light
author: Ancient Egyptian Priests and Scribes
significance: Provides crucial insight into ancient Egyptian beliefs about the afterlife, solar theology, and the pharaoh's role in maintaining cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Litany of Re emerged during the New Kingdom, a period of significant religious and artistic development in ancient Egypt. It is closely tied to the Osirian myth cycle, where the deceased king identified with Osiris, the god of the underworld, and sought resurrection. However, the Litany shifts the focus to Re, the sun god, emphasizing the pharaoh's solar transformation and eternal journey. The text describes Re's perilous voyage through the Duat, battling the forces of chaos and darkness, symbolized by the serpent Apophis. Each hour of the night presents unique challenges and requires Re's transformation into different forms to overcome them. The successful completion of this journey ensures the sun's rebirth each morning and, by extension, the deceased king's resurrection.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Litany of Re played a vital role in royal funerary rituals. It was inscribed on the walls of pharaohs' tombs, serving as a guide and protective spell for their journey through the afterlife. The text was recited by priests during funerary ceremonies, invoking Re's power and ensuring the deceased king's safe passage. By identifying with Re and reciting his names and forms, the pharaoh hoped to achieve union with the sun god and attain eternal life. The Litany also reinforced the pharaoh's divine status and his connection to the sun, the ultimate source of life and power. It solidified the belief that the pharaoh's well-being was intrinsically linked to the sun's cycle and the maintenance of cosmic order.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Litany of Re is rich in symbolism, reflecting the complex cosmology of ancient Egypt. Re's journey through the Duat symbolizes the cycle of death and rebirth, the struggle between order and chaos, and the triumph of light over darkness. The twelve hours of the night represent the different stages of the afterlife journey, each with its own unique challenges and transformations. Re's seventy-five forms embody the various aspects of the sun god's power and his ability to adapt to different situations. The serpent Apophis symbolizes the forces of chaos and destruction that constantly threaten the cosmic order. The Litany's emphasis on reciting names and epithets reflects the ancient Egyptian belief in the power of words and the ability to influence reality through language.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: While primarily a religious text of ancient Egypt, the Litany of Re continues to fascinate scholars and influence contemporary culture. Its themes of death, rebirth, and the cyclical nature of time resonate with modern audiences. The Litany has been studied extensively by Egyptologists, providing valuable insights into ancient Egyptian religion, cosmology, and funerary practices. Its imagery and symbolism have inspired artists, writers, and filmmakers, who have incorporated elements of the Litany into their works. The text also serves as a reminder of the enduring power of ancient Egyptian civilization and its profound impact on human history.

sources.author: Erik Hornung
sources.work: The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife
sources.description: Comprehensive analysis of Egyptian funerary texts, including the Litany of Re.

sources.author: Theodore H. Breen
sources.description: Discusses the role of religious texts in shaping cultural values and beliefs.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Re is the sun god, the source of light and life.

associations.name: Ma'at
associations.significance: The Litany reinforces Ma'at, the principle of cosmic order and balance.

associations.name: Duat
associations.significance: The underworld through which Re travels each night.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Uraeus
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Uraeus is similar to the Greek Oroboros, a snake eating its tail, symbolizing cyclical renewal and eternity.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Yggdrasil
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Similar to the world tree of Norse mythology, Yggdrasil, that connects all the nine realms.

companions.name: Ra
companions.description: Primordial sun god, the main deity to whom the Litany is dedicated.

companions.name: Osiris
companions.description: God of the afterlife, representing the deceased king's transformation and rebirth.

companions.name: Apophis
companions.description: The serpent of chaos who attempts to thwart Re's journey through the underworld.

worshipPractices.name: Recitation
worshipPractices.description: Priests recited the Litany of Re during funerary rituals to ensure the deceased king's safe passage to the afterlife.

worshipPractices.name: Inscriptions
worshipPractices.description: The text was inscribed on the walls of royal tombs, serving as a guide and protective spell for the deceased.

festivals.name: Festival of Ra
festivals.description: Festivals dedicated to Ra celebrated his power and ensured the continuation of the solar cycle.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Litany of Re continues to be studied by Egyptologists and inspires artists and writers interested in ancient Egyptian religion and mythology. Themes of death, rebirth, and the cyclical nature of time resonate in modern interpretations.
